turns.text: Hello Charolette, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. I... I appreciate you saying that about no right or wrong answers. Honestly, that's kind of rare to hear from someone in healthcare, so it helps a little.

*takes a breath*

I guess the thing is, I'm not actually pregnant right now. I'm trying to get there—or at least, I'm getting my body ready for it. After my first pregnancy didn't work out, there was this moment where I had to decide whether to keep hoping or just... give up. I chose to keep hoping, but some days that feels stupid.

*looks down at hands*

The hard part isn't even the medical stuff, which probably sounds weird coming from someone who's studying to be a nurse. I know what tests I need, I know what the numbers mean. But knowing all that doesn't make sitting in those waiting rooms any easier. And it doesn't fix the fact that I'm doing this alone—no partner, my family's not really in the picture, and I'm barely keeping my head above water financially.

*meets eyes briefly*

The clinics are... fine, I guess. The doctors are competent. But I feel like they see "23-year-old, previous loss, medium risk" and they just check boxes. Sometimes I want someone to actually ask me how I'm doing emotionally, you know? Not just about my labs.

I'm sorry, that was a lot to dump upfront. Where would you like me to start?
When you think about your goals for starting or growing your family, what were the most important outcomes you hoped to achieve — for yourself, your relationship, and your future child?

*pauses, a slight tension crossing face*

Okay, so... the relationship part is a little complicated for me to talk about, because there isn't one. And I know that's probably not what you were expecting to hear.

*fidgets with sleeve*

I think what I want most is just to be a mom. Like, genuinely—I've always wanted that. Even back in high school, I gravitated toward working with kids, which is part of why neonatal nursing makes so much sense to me. I want to understand my own child's medical needs. I want to be there for all of it.

For myself? I want to prove that my body can do this. That it won't fail me again. After the first loss, there's this voice in my head telling me I'm broken somehow, you know? So succeeding at pregnancy—actually carrying a baby to term—would quiet that voice a little.

*looks up*

And honestly? I want stability first. A full-time job, a place that feels secure, maybe even a partner eventually. I'm not naive—I know raising a kid alone is going to be hard. The bus rides alone are already exhausting me, and that's just to get to the clinic. But I also know I can't wait around forever hoping my life becomes perfect, because it might not.

*voice softens*

I just want my kid to have a mom who's present and okay. Not struggling on the edge all the time. Is that selfish? I don't know anymore.

When you think about your motivations for seeking preconception care or health advice before becoming pregnant, what was it that prompted you to take action — or, if you did not seek any care, what held you back?

*leans back, considering the question carefully*

I did seek it out, actually. After everything that happened with my first pregnancy, I couldn't just... pretend it didn't matter. I needed to know if there was something wrong with me, or if it was just bad luck, or what. So I made the appointments.

*runs hand through hair*

But I want to be honest—it wasn't like some perfectly motivated decision. It was more like... desperation mixed with my nursing brain being like, "Okay, Charolette, you need labs, you need to check your immunity status, you need your iron levels." So I started going through the motions.

The problem is, once I started seeking care, the logistics became this whole other beast. The clinic isn't exactly around the corner. Twenty minutes to the bus stop, then the actual ride... it adds up. And financially? Every appointment is money I'm trying to save for when I actually am pregnant. Copays, time off work if I can find work—it all just compounds.

*voice gets quieter*

But I think what really prompted me was fear. Fear that if I didn't do this, I'd regret it forever. Fear that I'm running out of time somehow, even though logically I know I'm only twenty-three. It's this contradiction where I'm simultaneously desperate to be ready and terrified I'll never be ready.

*looks directly at you*

Does that make sense? Like, I'm here because I have to be. Because the alternative is giving up, and I can't do that.

When you think about how you actually behaved during the preconception period — your lifestyle choices, health actions, information-seeking — what did you do, and how did those behaviours reflect what you truly believed was important?

*shifts uncomfortably, picking at a thread on my sleeve*

Oh man, okay. So there's like... what I know I *should* be doing, and then there's what I'm actually managing to do. And they're not always the same thing.

*sighs*

I'm obsessive about certain things. I track my cycle religiously—like, I have spreadsheets. I know exactly when I'm ovulating, what my patterns are. I take my vitamins, I got the titers checked, the iron panel. I read everything about neonatal care because that's already my world anyway. That part comes naturally.

But the other stuff? *laughs quietly* I'm a nursing student living paycheck to paycheck with no job right now. So eating perfectly healthy? Sometimes I'm grabbing whatever's cheapest at the store. Exercise? I walk to the bus stop and back. That's kind of my cardio. Sleep? *voice gets smaller* I stress-spiral at night thinking about everything, so that's not great either.

*meets your eyes*

I think what it shows is that I'm willing to do the medical stuff—the clinical stuff—because that feels like control. That feels like I'm doing something. But I'm also barely holding it together in other ways, and sometimes I wonder if the doctors notice that and just... don't say anything because they see I understand the science.

*voice wavers slightly*

I want to be the perfect candidate for pregnancy. But I'm also just trying to survive right now. Those two things don't always line up.
Can you walk me through a specific thing you did or changed during that period and what led you to that particular action?

*takes a moment, thinking*

Okay, so... the prenatal vitamins. That was the first concrete thing I did after my loss.

*fidgets with hands*

I remember sitting in my apartment after everything ended, and I just felt this need to do *something*. Like, if I could just take a pill, maybe I could prevent it from happening again. So I went to the drugstore and bought prenatal vitamins. The folic acid was supposed to help—I knew that from my coursework—but honestly? A lot of it was just... magical thinking, you know?

*voice gets quieter*

I started taking them every single morning. No matter what. Even when I couldn't afford other things, I made sure I had those. It became this ritual, this way of telling myself that I was preparing, that I was serious about this. Like the vitamin was a contract I was making with my body.

*looks up*

But here's the thing—I started doing it before I even had my first appointment with the clinic. I didn't ask a doctor if I should. I just... did it. Because in my head, if I was already taking care of myself, maybe God or the universe or whatever would take pity on me and not let it happen again.

*slight laugh, tinged with sadness*

Which is funny because I'm supposed to be this science-minded person, and yet I'm basically relying on superstition. But when you're scared and alone, sometimes the vitamin bottle feels like the only thing you can control.

When you think about your emotional experience during the preconception period — the waiting, the uncertainty, and the anticipation — what feelings stand out most strongly, and were any of those feelings hard to acknowledge or talk about?

*goes very quiet, staring at hands for a long moment*

The hardest one is anger. I didn't expect that.

*voice tightens slightly*

I thought I'd feel hopeful, or sad about what I lost, or anxious about the future. And I do feel those things. But there's this rage underneath it all that I don't really know what to do with. I'm angry at my body for not working the first time. I'm angry that I don't have a partner to share this with, that I'm doing this alone. I'm angry at people who get pregnant easily, who don't have to think about it.

*pauses, breathing*

And I'm angry at myself for being angry, which is stupid. Like, I feel guilty for not being more positive about it all. I'm a nursing student—I'm supposed to understand physiological processes, supposed to be rational about this. But sometimes I just want to scream that it's not fair.

*looks up, eyes slightly glossy*

The thing I don't really talk about is the shame. When people ask why I don't have a partner, or when I'm sitting in that waiting room surrounded by couples... there's this feeling like I'm doing something wrong. Like my life is supposed to look different at twenty-three. And I know logically that's not true, but the feeling is there anyway.

*voice cracks slightly*

And the loneliness. God, the loneliness is so heavy. I can't talk to my family about it. I don't really have close friends here. So I just... carry it by myself. That's the hardest part to admit out loud.
Can you describe a specific moment when those feelings were particularly intense — what was happening and how did you respond?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting watery*

Yeah. There was... there was this moment about two weeks ago, actually.

*voice becomes quieter*

I was sitting in the waiting room at the clinic, waiting for my appointment. And this woman came in with her partner—they were holding hands, both looking at her belly like it was the most precious thing in the world. And she was probably only a few weeks along, you know? Early pregnancy glow, the guy was being all protective and sweet.

*swallows hard*

And I just... broke. Like, not visibly. I didn't cry or anything. But something inside me just cracked. I was sitting there in my own clothes that don't fit right because I've lost weight from stress, no one holding my hand, waiting to get blood drawn again to see if my body is *finally* cooperating, and I watched this couple be so *happy* about theirs.

*wipes eyes quickly*

I went to the bathroom after my appointment and I just sat in a stall for like twenty minutes. Not even crying, just... sitting there feeling all of it at once. The anger, the sadness, the shame that I was angry at someone else's happiness. The fear that I'd never get to have that—someone excited about *my* baby.

*voice wavers*

And then I had to catch the bus home and pretend everything was fine because I had to study for a pharmacology exam. So I just... swallowed it all back down.

*looks at you*

That's kind of my default. Feel it intensely, then bury it.

When you think about the barriers you encountered — or anticipated — in your path to conception or early pregnancy preparation, what were the most significant obstacles, and how did you navigate them?

*leans forward, rubbing face*

Okay, where do I even start? Because there are so many.

*counts on fingers*

The transportation thing is huge. I don't have a car. The clinic is not on a direct bus line, so it's this whole production every time. Twenty minutes walking, then waiting for the bus, then the ride itself. In winter, in Massachusetts, that's brutal. And once I'm actually pregnant? I'm terrified. What if I'm sick and can't make it to the bus stop? What if I go into labor and have to figure out how to get to the hospital? I've literally thought through worst-case scenarios.

*voice gets frustrated*

Then there's money. I'm not working right now—like, I'm looking, but nothing's solid yet. So every copay at the clinic is money I don't have. And I know once I'm pregnant, I need to be able to afford prenatal care, delivery, everything after. The stress of that alone keeps me up at night.

*pauses*

And the isolation. I don't have family support here. No partner. My friends are scattered, and they don't really understand what I'm going through. The clinic staff—they're professional, but they don't *know* me. So when I'm sitting there terrified, there's no one to just hold my hand and say it'll be okay.

*voice quieter*

As for navigating it? Honestly? I'm not sure I am. I'm just pushing through. I take the bus. I skip meals sometimes to save money for copays. I pretend I'm fine when I'm not.

*meets your eyes*

I don't know if that's navigating or just surviving.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits back, thinking carefully*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*takes a breath*

I want the healthcare system to understand that being a nursing student doesn't make this easier. If anything, it makes it harder because I know too much. I know what can go wrong. I know the statistics. And when a doctor talks to me in shorthand because they assume I understand everything, it actually makes me feel more isolated, not less.

*voice gets firmer*

But also... I wish someone had asked me about my support system earlier. Like, really asked. Not just "do you have family nearby?" but actually listened to what that means—that I'm doing this completely alone. Because that changes things. That changes how I approach every single decision.

*pauses, vulnerability showing*

And I guess I want people to know that just because I'm young and educated and I understand the medical stuff doesn't mean I have my life figured out. I'm terrified. I'm alone. I'm barely making it financially. And I still want this baby so badly it physically hurts sometimes.

*voice wavers slightly*

If this research helps make things better for people like me—people without partners, without family, without stable jobs—that would mean something. Because right now, I feel like the system is kind of built for people who have more than I do.

*small, sad smile*

That's all, I think. Thank you for actually listening.
